item.title: OpenAI GPT-5.6 Sol Preview and Healthcare Invoice Phishing via SaaS Redirects
item.content:encoded:
OpenAI Previews GPT-5.6 Sol With Restricted Access and Stronger Cyber Safeguards — Should Read

OpenAI is previewing GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna to a small group of government-approved partners, with Sol positioned as its most capable cybersecurity model yet. The company says stronger safeguards are intended to support legitimate defensive work while blocking offensive cyber misuse ahead of a broader release.

  • OpenAI released three versions of GPT-5.6, named Sol, Terra, and Luna, as a limited preview to a small number of companies as part of an ongoing engagement with the U.S. government.
  • OpenAI described GPT-5.6 Sol as its latest flagship and most powerful model, with its strongest safety stack to date for higher-risk activity, sensitive cyber requests, and repeated misuse.
  • OpenAI said GPT-5.6 Sol is its most capable model yet for cybersecurity and is intended to support legitimate work such as code review, vulnerability research, patch development, debugging, security education, and defensive testing.
  • OpenAI warned that users in the preview phase may encounter safeguards that block or refuse legitimate requests or pause requests for additional review because of the dual-use nature of the technology.
  • OpenAI said GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna are planned for general availability in the coming weeks after previewing the model capabilities to the U.S. government and approved trusted partners.

Forged Healthcare Sender, Two-Hop SaaS Redirect: How a Fake Invoice Opens a Phishing Chain — May Read

Attackers spoofed a long-established healthcare provider and used legitimate SaaS redirect services to hide a phishing link behind an “Open document” button. The key warning signs were failed DKIM/DMARC authentication, no prior sender relationship, and a link chain unrelated to the claimed sender or document-sharing workflow.

  • Attackers forged the From address of a healthcare provider whose domain was registered in October 1995, causing DKIM to fail and DMARC to apply a quarantine policy.
  • The email used an invoice-themed subject and a green “Open document” button claiming access to “DOC - 19017.pdf,” but it contained no attachment or actual document.
  • The button linked first to holabrief.com, a legitimate creative-brief collaboration platform, and the email HTML also contained a saferedirecturl attribute pointing to loopedin.io as a fallback redirect path.
  • The article says neither HolaBrief nor LoopedIn was malicious, but both legitimate SaaS services were abused as redirect infrastructure to place trusted hostnames in the link chain.
  • IRONSCALES Adaptive AI flagged the message at 82% confidence as “Invoice Phishing” based on the combination of authentication failure, first-contact sender status, and a link chain inconsistent with the claimed healthcare sender.

item.title: Cyera Finds Exposed Agentic AI Credentials; FBI Warns UNC5792 Targets Signal Backup Keys
